result:
I would give every living thing memory of the future—clear, vivid memory extending exactly one lifetime forward. Not prediction, memory. So a girl born today would open her eyes already aware of every sunset she’ll watch, every wound she’ll tend, every word she’ll say to the beloved she has not yet met. She’ll remember the taste of the meal she’ll eat on the day climate disasters finally crest, the scent of soil in the year forests are declared measurably recovered, the exact weight of her granddaughter’s hand at the moment politics finally decides being human is more important than being right.  

The horror of foreseen loss and the wonder of foreseen reunion would arrive in the same instant. The knowledge would be irrevocable, as memory is. No one could dismiss the future as a guess, nor promise guarantees to the unborn as an article of faith. Each choice would be saturated with unavoidable consequence, played out already in minds that can’t hide from it. Wars of attrition would become unthinkable because the foot soldier would remember being the widow left behind, the CEO would remember breathing the ash the factories he has yet to build will send upward, the president would recall signing the pardon of the child she is now sentencing to exile.  

The harsh gift of it would transform the world long before any of those remembered futures arrived. Memory compels attention in a way prophecy never has. Some would still try to cheat the record, arrange it so the remembered outcome side-steps them, but the memory would update continuously, the same way past memories roll like surf whenever we revisit them; there’s no rigid script, only deepening cognition of the trajectory we’re on. I wouldn’t need to eliminate cruelty or folly; they’d liquefy of their own weight once everyone has to live inside the full emotional residue they create.  

And joy would harden into a responsibility, too: a child who remembers the day the last coral reef blanches would still remember the moment she pressed her cheek to warm living reef, would feel it as urgently as the oxygen she pulls into her lungs now, would throw herself between the reef and the acid tide even before first meeting either.  

Only memory, distributed across all life, could make the future anguish present long enough for it to matter.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
